skill.name: secrets-management
skill.description: Guides teams through CI/CD secrets management with Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, cloud key vaults, rotation workflows, and secret scanning.
skill.summary: Plan and implement safer secrets handling across CI/CD pipelines and cloud platforms.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly true detections of shell, CI, cloud, network, and secret-handling examples inside SKILL.md, but I found no executable skill code or confirmed malicious exfiltration. The main risk is that users may copy examples that echo secrets, rely on long-lived cloud credentials, or use dummy secrets in unsafe ways.

medium_findings.title: Examples Echo Secrets Into CI Logs
medium_findings.description: The GitHub Actions and GitLab examples print API keys or database URLs. This is a true positive for risky secret handling because copied examples could expose credentials in job logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly echo secret variables. They are documentation snippets, not active code, so the risk depends on users copying them.

medium_findings.title: Long-Lived AWS Credentials Recommended For CI
medium_findings.description: The GitHub Actions example uses AWS access key and secret access key values from repository secrets. This is functional, but OIDC federation is safer for CI/CD access to AWS.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The snippet clearly configures static AWS credentials in CI. It may be legitimate, but it raises avoidable credential lifetime and storage risk.

medium_findings.title: Dummy Secrets May Encourage Unsafe Copying
medium_findings.description: Several examples include root tokens, simple passwords, and placeholder production names. These are likely instructional placeholders, but they need stronger warnings and non-sensitive placeholders.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values appear in documentation examples and are not evidence of real leaked credentials. They still create a user safety issue if copied into production.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs And Local IP Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded Vault URLs and localhost address are true pattern matches, but they are example endpoints in Markdown. I found no evidence that the skill sends data to those endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are static configuration examples inside SKILL.md. No executable file or exfiltration destination was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged weak cryptography near the description and related skills text. I found no cryptographic algorithm use at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive text and a related skill name, not crypto code. This is a clear pattern false positive.

low_findings.title: Critical Combined-Heuristic Finding Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The combined code execution, network, and credential heuristic is explained by documentation examples for a secrets-management skill. I found no active script, prompt injection, or malicious data flow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risk factors co-occur in Markdown snippets that teach secret tooling. The absence of executable skill files lowers confidence in malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Runnable Shell And CI Snippets
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains copyable shell, GitHub Actions, GitLab, Terraform, Kubernetes, and Docker examples. These are expected for the topic, but they need careful review before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command patterns are real, but they are examples rather than hidden execution paths. User review is still important because they touch secrets and local files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret Values Exported To Environment Variables
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples retrieve secrets and place them in process or CI environment variables. This is common, but it can leak through logs, subprocesses, or artifact capture.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The snippets explicitly assign secret values to shell or CI environment variables. The risk is contextual but well established.

content.value_statement: Teams often leak secrets through source control, CI logs, and overly broad credentials. This skill gives structured guidance for Vault, cloud secret managers, rotation, access control, and scanning.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to choose a secrets backend for CI/CD pipelines.
Provides examples for Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GitHub Secrets, GitLab variables, and Kubernetes External Secrets.
Outlines secret rotation steps and an AWS rotation workflow.
Lists best practices for masking, audit logging, least privilege, and scanning.
Shows secret scanning examples with TruffleHog in pre-commit and CI workflows.
limitations: The skill is guidance only and does not provision infrastructure by itself.
Some examples use placeholders that require hardening before production use.
It does not cover every cloud provider or enterprise vault product.
It does not validate whether existing credentials are already compromised.

use_cases.title: Harden Deployment Pipelines
use_cases.description: Design safer secret retrieval, masking, and access control for deployment jobs.

use_cases.title: Plan Secret Rotation
use_cases.description: Create a repeatable rotation process for database passwords and API tokens.

use_cases.title: Review Platform Secret Storage
use_cases.description: Compare native CI secrets, Vault, and cloud secret managers for a project.

prompt_templates.title: Choose A Secrets Backend
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a secrets backend for a CI/CD pipeline. Compare Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GitHub Secrets, and GitLab variables for my project.

prompt_templates.title: Harden A Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this CI/CD workflow for secret handling risks. Suggest safer retrieval, masking, least-privilege access, and logging practices.

prompt_templates.title: Design Rotation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a secret rotation plan for database credentials used by production services. Include rollout, validation, rollback, and audit requirements.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Secret Governance Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a secrets management standard for multiple teams. Include ownership, naming, access reviews, rotation frequency, scanning, incident response, and exceptions.

output_examples.input: Review our GitHub Actions deployment workflow for secret exposure.
output_examples.output: A prioritized review that identifies log exposure, long-lived credentials, missing masking, and recommended changes such as OIDC and scoped permissions.

output_examples.input: Plan rotation for a production database password stored in a cloud secret manager.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step rotation plan covering new credential creation, application update, validation, old credential revocation, monitoring, and audit logging.

output_examples.input: Compare Vault with native CI secrets for a small engineering team.
output_examples.output: A practical comparison of setup effort, access control, audit features, rotation support, operational burden, and when each option is appropriate.

best_practices: Use short-lived, scoped credentials whenever the platform supports them.
Mask secrets in logs and avoid printing secret-derived values.
Pair secret storage with rotation, audit logging, access reviews, and scanning.
anti_patterns: Do not copy placeholder passwords, root tokens, or example keys into real environments.
Do not echo API keys, database URLs, or generated secrets in CI logs.
Do not use broad long-lived cloud credentials when federated identity is available.

faq.question: Does this skill store or retrieve my secrets?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples, but it does not run a secrets backend or access your credentials.

faq.question: Can I use the examples directly in production?
faq.answer: Review and harden them first. Replace placeholders, remove secret logging, and apply least-privilege access.

faq.question: Which tools does the skill discuss?
faq.answer: It covers Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, Google Secret Manager, GitHub Secrets, GitLab variables, Kubernetes External Secrets, and TruffleHog.

faq.question: Does it support secret rotation planning?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes manual rotation steps and an AWS-oriented automated rotation workflow.

faq.question: Why is the security risk rated medium?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation, but some copyable examples show risky patterns such as printing secrets in CI logs.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
